A sustainable approach in water desalination with the integration of renewable energy sources: Environmental engineering challenges and perspectives
====================================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Aref Shokri a, Mahdi Sanavi Fard b

Abstract
--------

For decades considerable attempt has been specifically devoted toward the integration of [renewable energy sources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/renewable-energy-source "Learn more about renewable energy sources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (RES) to power different [desalination](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/desalination "Learn more about desalination from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") systems. However, at present, various techno-economic limitations hamper directing these efforts towards wide-range applications. In this review paper, the current status of RES-based [desalination technologies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/desalination-technology "Learn more about desalination technologies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), with a focus on the [hybrid process](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/hybrid-process "Learn more about hybrid process from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), their enhancement, and promising application, besides their current techno-economic restrictions has been explored. This approach is highly eco-friendly since waste formation is the minimum and can be a potential advantage to the construction of local industrial plants. In addition, it can offer effective solutions in the agriculture field like crop irrigation. Nevertheless, it is exigent to enhance their technical-thermal performance by conducting a comprehensive analysis for industrializing them. For maximizing the [synergistic effect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/synergistic-effect "Learn more about synergistic effect from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of integrated RES modification of traditional [desalination systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/desalination-system "Learn more about desalination systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with an incessant pursuit for research and development is absolutely crucial. Because, this approach significantly underpins their market penetration and feasibility in a cost-effective manner. Moreover, the authors briefly presented an overview of environmental prospects and engineering challenges of desalination for designing a sustainable methodology in the water-energy nexus. Also, the authors propose the knowledge gaps and perspectives to delineate the future research direction concerning environmental-engineering-energy aspects. All in all, despite several outstanding advancements in the integration of RES with various desalination technologies, their long-term performance and energy-intensive nature still remain challenging. Hence it can be concluded that both desalination technologies and RES still require further intensive research efforts for evaluating their long-term conduction, minimizing energy consumption, and mitigation of [environmental footprints](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/environmental-footprint "Learn more about environmental footprints from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

Renewable energy source

Water desalination

Sustainable technology

Reverse osmosis

Integrated energy source and desalination technologies

1. Introduction
---------------

Energy and water sources are considered crucial elements which are indispensable for incessant human life. Due to the apparent significance of water and energy to human beings, the energy-water nexus has achieved considerable attention recently. With gradually increasing the world's population, the exigent requirement for both fresh water and energy supply will be inevitable; as a result, posing significant pressure on the present utilities designed for fresh water and energy supply ([Nwulu and Fahrioglu,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0105)). Also, due to the fact that two-thirds of the earth consists of water, the ecosystem of the earth significantly depends on the availability of water sources; however, only one percent of those water sources are proper for human usage ([Abdelkareem et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0001)). Currently, around 20% of the earth population severely suffers a water shortage challenge, and out of which, approximately 25% have accessibility to [water resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/water-resources "Learn more about water resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Xiao-jun et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0147)).

The [desalination](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/desalination "Learn more about desalination from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a type of purification process for achieving drinkable freshwater from vastly available saline sources such as brackish or [seawater](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/seawater "Learn more about seawater from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") water. The desalination process during the recent decades has undergone continuous improvement, causing the emergence of various technologies, which can mainly be classified into thermal and membrane methods. However, there are some other methods like ion exchange (I.Ex), liquid-liquid extraction (LLE), and [gas hydrate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/gas-hydrate "Learn more about gas hydrate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (G.Hyd). Thermal technologies as its name imply utilize heat for their operation. Generally, [thermal desalination](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/thermal-desalination "Learn more about thermal desalination from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") involves heating hyper-saline water and gathering condensed vapor for the production of freshwater. Multi-stage flash (MSF), multi-effect distillation (MED), [vapor compression](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/vapor-compression "Learn more about vapor compression from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") distillation (VCD), adsorption desalination (AD), humidification-dehumidification (HDH), and freezing are classified under the thermal methods category. The membrane-based desalination process utilizes membrane and electrical energy for powering its process. They are mainly classified into [forward osmosis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/forward-osmosis "Learn more about forward osmosis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (FO), [electrodialysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrodialysis "Learn more about electrodialysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (ED) and [electrodialysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/electrodialysis "Learn more about electrodialysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") reversal (EDR), [reverse osmosis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/reverse-osmosis "Learn more about reverse osmosis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (RO), and nano-filtration (NF). The fundamentals of osmosis are that water is transported from a low concentration to a high concentration aqueous environment by a semi-permeable membrane, thus reducing the concentration until achieving enough amount of stability in concentration ([Biesheuvel et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0018)). RO, MSF, and MED are regarded as the most commonly used technologies [(Ahmed et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0005); [Jones et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0075)). [Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#fig0001) demonstrates the percent contribution of different desalination methods ([Jones et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0075)). RO technology monopolizes the desalination market in comparison with other membrane methods.

![Image 12: Fig 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666765722001168-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (441KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666765722001168-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (441KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666765722001168-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig 1. Ability of different desalination technologies for decreasing salinity content from feedwater ([Mehmood and Ren,2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0097)).

The ability of different [desalination technologies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/desalination-technology "Learn more about desalination technologies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for decreasing salinity from feedwater and input electrical energy requirement for each technology was presented in [Figs 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#fig0001) and [2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#fig0002), respectively. As it is evident from [Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#fig0001) the highest and lowest amount of produced water salinity is related to the HDH and FO technologies, respectively. According to [Fig.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#fig0002), the highest and lowest amount of electrical energy is related to the freezing and [HDH technologies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/dehumidification-technology "Learn more about HDH technologies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), respectively.

![Image 13: Fig 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666765722001168-gr2.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (344KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666765722001168-gr2_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (344KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666765722001168-gr2.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig 2. Input electrical energy requirement for different desalination technologies ([Mehmood and Ren,2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0097)).

In spite of the recent advancement in desalination technologies, they are considered energy-intensive; hence effective solutions are needed to decrease operational costs and therefore enhance the [economic feasibility](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/economic-feasibility "Learn more about economic feasibility from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of these processes. Moreover, traditional energy sources driven by [fossil fuels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/fossil-fuel "Learn more about fossil fuels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have environmental adverse effects, which result in contaminant and greenhouse gas emissions ([Gbadamosi et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0047)a, [2018b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0048)). The application of [renewable energy sources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/renewable-energy-source "Learn more about renewable energy sources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (RES) is one of the viable solutions ([Gude,2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0061); [Nassrullah et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0104)), which due to its critical effect in dealing with climatic variations, plays a [pivotal role](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/pivotal-role "Learn more about pivotal role from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Ali et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0007); [Brendel et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0021); [Godart,2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0056)). However, they have significant restrictions, like intermittent wind speed and solar radiation. The integrated process is typically utilized for addressing such limitations which, presents highly effective results ([Ghaffour et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0053)). In areas that face water shortage and significant solar radiation, integrated wind-based or solar-based process demonstrate great potential. An exhaustive investigation is required to ensure the sustainable integrations of RES, especially in areas that struggle with significant water shortages ([Tsai et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0138)). It is imperative to recognize cost-effective technologies which rely on RES to accelerate the long-run conduction of [desalination systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/desalination-system "Learn more about desalination systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

As the methodology of the current review paper, papers in the blueprint stage were selected by appropriate keywords from Google Scholar source as the most reliable research database. Some of the used keywords for selection were: integration of renewable energy sources in desalination, scaling in desalination, [membrane fouling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/membrane-fouling "Learn more about membrane fouling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in desalination, engineering challenges of desalination, and the like. The emphasis was given to the new and up to dated papers concerning the aforementioned keywords mostly from 2018 to 2022. However, older papers suitably were taken into account as in some conditions they have contained valuable data and solutions. Another inclusion criterion was to focus on papers that deeply and critically presented future research challenges and perspectives. All resultant papers were carefully scrutinized based on originality rate and discussion of required data. Finally, after sorting the most important and relevant information, they have critically analyzed and summarized.

To our best knowledge, there is not any unique review paper concerning simultaneous water-energy nexus and environmental-engineering challenges of desalination like fouling/scaling, brine production, and greenhouse gas emissions conducted. Hence, the authors in the present paper comprehensively discussed different individual and integrated RES-based desalination systems. The major limitations regarding RES were addressed. Moreover, [environmental footprints](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/environmental-footprint "Learn more about environmental footprints from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and engineering challenges of desalination are explored. Finally, future research perspectives are presented to bridge knowledge gaps toward a sustainable future in the water-energy nexus and dramatic flourishing in this field.

2. Renewable energy sources for desalination
--------------------------------------------

Every [desalination technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/desalination-technology "Learn more about desalination technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") requires different types of energy to operate its process. Traditional energy sources have different restrictions, like adverse environmental effects; as a result, propelling the researchers toward the application of RES for operating [desalination](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/desalination "Learn more about desalination from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") plants. Hence, various desalination designs for applying RES as a promising solution are investigated and still ongoing. According to the explorations of [Abdelkareem et al.(2018)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0001), the selection of proper RES for desalination is based on different parameters, like the [economic feasibility](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/economic-feasibility "Learn more about economic feasibility from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of freshwater production, feedwater properties and pressure, location, and desalination plant size. Different types of RES for integration with the [desalination systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/desalination-system "Learn more about desalination systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are presented in the following sections.

### 2.1. Geothermal energy

[Geothermal energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/geothermal-energy "Learn more about Geothermal energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is the heat that is derived from beneath the earth, in the type of either steam or [hot water](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/hot-water "Learn more about hot water from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or a combination of them. Geothermal energy is classified into temperatures below 150°C or low temperatures and temperatures higher than 150°C or high temperatures ([Al-Karaghouli and Kazmerski,2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0006)). Generally, [geothermal wells](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/geothermal-well "Learn more about geothermal wells from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with a depth of 100 m can be safely utilized for the desalination process. Geothermal energy is a highly stable and eco-friendly RES. It offers regular heat flux, which renders it a remarkably reliable energy source. As a result, in the application of geothermal energy to [desalination plants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/desalination-plant "Learn more about desalination plants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), the requirement for heat storage facilities is precluded. Typically, geothermal energy shows economic feasibility over solar energy sources ([Ghaffour et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0050)). As a result, in regions with the availability of enough geothermal energy, this energy source can be highly beneficial, under the condition that by techno-economic evaluation its validity is confirmed ([Bundschuh et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0022)). Moreover, since its operating costs are fairly low and also do not rely on weather fluctuations, its viability is ensured. However, the main restriction is the significant cost of power plants and the limited location for their activity ([Abdelkareem et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0001)). The geothermal plant is highly practical for [thermal desalination](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/thermal-desalination "Learn more about thermal desalination from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") operations because it offers a direct provision of [heat energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/thermal-energy "Learn more about heat energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). In other words, geothermal energy sources with temperatures varying from 70 to 90°C are available in large quantities over the world which is preferable for the [MED](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/multi-effect-distillation "Learn more about MED from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") process because it requires lower ranges of temperature for operation. Moreover, it can be converted to electrical and mechanical energy for application in [membrane desalination](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/desalination-membrane "Learn more about membrane desalination from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") operations. For example, in [RO](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/reverse-osmosis "Learn more about RO from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), mechanical energy is utilized in the pressure pump, which produces the required pressure to propel the feedwater towards the membrane. The low-temperature feedwater has a higher viscosity which requires higher pressure for the [RO](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/reverse-osmosis "Learn more about RO from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") operations and, as a result, demands higher energy usage. Operating RO with a temperature tolerance varying from 20 to 35°C, [geothermal waters](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/geothermal-water "Learn more about geothermal waters from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with a proper salinity and temperature can be used as feed to generate freshwater. In this way, the required mechanical energy to pump [cold water](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/cold-water "Learn more about cold water from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") during the winter to some extent alleviated ([Gude,2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0061)). Regardless of the two aforesaid methods, other techniques are available in which hot water is directed from the ground towards the circulation of energy-producing turbines and then goes to the MED plants. In this way, geothermal energy can be applied effectively to the operation of desalination plants, especially in areas with hyper-saline waters ([Esmaeilion,2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0037)). In addition, not only geothermal energy can be utilized for operating desalination plants but also it can be properly treated if it has an acceptable amount of chemical components ([Kabay et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0077)). Then this treated water can be utilized for crop irrigation or even serve as [drinking water](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/potable-water "Learn more about drinking water from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Çermikli et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0024)).

Significant quantities of energy can be saved by [geothermal heating](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/geothermal-heating "Learn more about geothermal heating from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") compared with other RES which is not feasible. The results based on the different kinds of desalination technologies are presented in [Fig.3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#fig0003) ([Gude,2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#bib0061)). As it is evident from [Fig.3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722001168#fig0003) between 46 to 89% of energy can be saved by the application of geothermal e

SEAWATER DESALINATION TECHNOLOGY
================================

The most widely used and cost-effective solution for providing water to serve the continuously increasing demand for new sources of freshwater for the growing population and ensure water security

[Contact an Expert](https://ide-tech.com/en/water-solutions/sea-water-desalination/#hcon)

![Image 4](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/sea_water_sec1.png)

What is Seawater Desalination?
------------------------------

The world is in need of fresh water. Water is fundamental to life, yet sustainable freshwater resources are increasingly limited in quantity and quality. Population growth, changing water consumption patterns, and climate change threaten our freshwater supplies.

Seawater desalination is the process of removal of salts and dissolved solids from seawater. It is an important and vital solution that can combat water scarcity and an alternative, feasible and reliable source of water to ensure water sustainability.

![Image 5](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/10/sea_water_sec2.jpg)

![Image 6](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/themes/ide/images/seawater_sec2_circle.svg)

![Image 7](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/themes/ide/images/heading_light_blue.svg)

### TYPES OF SEAWATER DESALINATION TECHNOLOGIES

There are two main types of seawater desalination technologies: membrane-based (RO) and thermal (MED, MVC). Reverse Osmosis (RO) desalination uses the principle of osmosis to remove salt and other impurities by transferring water through a series of semi-permeable membranes. Thermal desalination uses heat, from power plants or refineries, to evaporate and condense water to purify it.

Each project and customer has its unique requirements and challenges that IDE strives to meet. A variety of factors come into play when selecting the appropriate solution for each project, the required capacity, quality of the source water, quality of the water produced, and energy requirements.

[Read more](javascript:;)

![Image 8](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/12/SWRO-DESALINATION-WORLD-LEADER-img.png)

![Image 9](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/themes/ide/images/white_heading.svg)

### IDE - A DESALINATION WORLD LEADER

IDE leads the water industry with some of the world’s most advanced thermal and membrane desalination plants. We have provided small to large cost-effective desalination solutions in _over_ 500 plants in 50 countries for _more than_ 6 decades. IDE works in partnership with a wide range of customers: municipalities, energy, mining, and power plants, on all aspects of water projects and delivers approximately 3 million m3/day of high-quality water worldwide.

![Image 10](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/10/sea_water_sec4.jpg)

![Image 11](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/themes/ide/images/circles-1-51.svg)

![Image 12](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/themes/ide/images/gal-04.svg)

### WHAT ARE THE BENEFITS OF SEAWATER DESALINATION?

Seawater desalination offers several benefits, making it a valuable solution to address water scarcity and ensure a reliable source of freshwater around the world:

– Additional Water Sources: Seawater desalination provides a sustainable and reliable source of freshwater, reducing dependence on rainfall surface water and groundwater, which are limited.

– Improved Water Quality: Seawater desalination produces high-quality freshwater that meets stringent drinking water standards.

– Environmental Conservation: By reducing the need for groundwater extraction or surface water diversion, seawater desalination can help protect natural ecosystems and reduce the ecological impact of water resource utilization.

– Drought Resilience: Seawater desalination provides a drought-resistant source of water, which is crucial for communities and industries.

– Economical growth: Desalinated water is suitable for various uses (municipal, industrial, agricultural, and others), thus supporting economic growth and quality of life.

![Image 13](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/10/sea_water_sec5.jpg)

![Image 14](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/themes/ide/images/seawater_sec5_circle.svg)

![Image 15](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/themes/ide/images/heading_light_blue.svg)

### ACHIEVING SUSTAINABLE SEAWATER DESALINATION

As water desalination plants expand globally, they are required to minimize their environmental impact and maximize their sustainability. Over the years, IDE proved that efficient and cost-effective desalination goes hand in hand with sustainability by implementing a variety of environmentally friendly technologies and design approaches.

IDE ensures that our desalination plants fit our customers’ economic and environmental goals.  We are continuously improving energy efficiency so as to minimize both Opex and carbon footprint of the plants . Through continual sustainable thinking and technological advancements, IDE has achieved some of the industry’s lowest energy consumption for desalination plants.

Desalination FAQ
----------------

[Which country desalinates the most water?](javascript:void(0);)

There are approximately 16,000 operational [desalination](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination) plants, located across 177 countries, which generate [an estimated 95 million m3/day of fresh water](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969718349167). Saudi Arabia is the country that relies most on desalination – mostly of seawater. The US is in second place. Per capita SA desalinates more, but the USA desalinates more water overall.

[What’s the difference between SWRO and BWRO?](javascript:void(0);)

SWRO stands for Seawater Reverse Osmosis, while BWRO stands for  Brackish water Reverse Osmosis. S3. Which industries use the most desalinated water?

Desalinated water is a broad term. In general, the power industry and refineries are where it is common to see desalination plants since they are close to the sea and have enough energy to support the desalination process. However, due to new regulations, more and more plants use desalination to reuse their water. They, therefore, provide high-quality water by desalinating treated municipal effluents to industries such as textiles, semiconductors, etc.

[Which industries use the most desalinated water?](javascript:void(0);)

Desalinated water is a broad term. In general, the power industry and refineries are where it is common to see desalination plants, since they are close to the sea and have enough energy to support the desalination process. However, due to new regulations, more and more plants use desalination to reuse their water. They therefore, provide high-quality water by desalinating treated municipal effluents to industries such as textile, semiconductors, etc.

[What are the advantages of seawater desalination?](javascript:void(0);)

The advantage of seawater desalination is that there is no limit to the size of the plant. In addition, proximity to the sea provides an unlimited quantity of feed water and can accept a high amount of concentrated water

[What is a desalination plant?](javascript:void(0);)

A desalination plant is a plant that uses the technology of separating salt from saline water and produces permeate water. This can be done using membrane, distillation, or Ion exchange technology, and more. The most common technology for desalination today is membrane technology.

[**Contact a desalination expert to learn more about IDE’s desalination plants.**](https://go.ide-tech.com/contact-an-expert)

[What are the environmental impacts of desalination?](javascript:void(0);)

Seawater desalination  involves some processes that could impact marine life. However, those impacts can be avoided or minimized by implementing environmental safeguards at every project phase. Therefore, every project needs to have an Environmental Impact Statement.

[What are the costs of desalination?](javascript:void(0);)

The cost of desalination can be broken down into the following elements:

*   Capital cost (CAPEX) (40 percent)
*   Labor (3 percent)
*   Membrane replacement (3 percent)
*   Maintenance and parts (7 percent)
*   Consumables (3 percent)
*   Electrical energy (44 percent)

Seawater Desalination has historically been perceived as a more expensive option compared with the traditional treatment of surface or groundwater, . However, one of the latest breakthroughs in desalination has been an improvement in the overall cost, including operational costs (OPEX) and the initial capital expenditure (CAPEX).  Very recently, project tenders in Abu Dhabi, Saudi Arabia, and Israel have seen the price fall to the range of  $0.50/m3 for the first time.

[How long does it take to build a plant, from the permit phase to the final construction phase?](javascript:void(0);)

The average time it takes to design, construct and commission a desalination plant varies by the size, geography and specifics of the plant. For large plants with a capacity of  \> 100,000 m3/day, 36 to 40 months can be expected. For smaller plants, it’s 15 to 24 months. Again it depends on the complexity of the plant

Wondering how long it took us to build some of our plants? Here are just a few numbers to think about:

1.  Sorek 1 150 Million cubic m per year -36 months
2.  Hadera 100 Million cubic m per year 36 months
3.  Afikey Mayim 6500 cubic m /day 18 months

[What happens to the salt that is removed from the water?](javascript:void(0);)

All desalination technologies have the same byproduct – concentrate or brine. The amount varies depending on the water source being desalinated. Seawater desalination plants discharge more brine – almost 50% of the feed water volume. This is less in brackish water desalination plants -where brine discharged is approximately  15% and 30% of the feed.

Increasing regulatory pressure, growing environmental awareness, and more and larger membrane desalination plants are driving the need for better brine minimization – one of the greatest challenges currently facing the water treatment sector.

The current brine minimization process uses a combination of 3 technologies – reverse osmosis, followed by thermal evaporation, followed by thermal crystallization. There are significant differences in the cost of these technologies.

[How can you improve the performance of an RO membrane?](javascript:void(0);)

As with any industrial facility, seawater reverse osmosis (SWRO) and brackish water reverse osmosis (BWRO) desalination plants are prone to wear and tear. If not appropriately managed, scaling and fouling are very common issues, leading to membrane clogging and even mechanical failure, affecting the plant’s performance, productivity, and profitability.

IDE’s **RO Membrane Management Program** (ROMMP) is the best way to keep a constant finger on the plant’s pulse, manage the highest value component of the SWRO system – the RO membranes, and ensure their optimal performance is maintained throughout the operation. Proper operation and maintenance of the RO system are paramount to guaranteeing long-term availability, operational efficiency, and minimal downtime.

IDE plants globally include more than 300,000 installed RO membranes  The  minimal replacement of membranes in our plants serves as a living testimony to the first-rate maintenance implemented in these plants .

[Is there funding available for desalination projects?](javascript:void(0);)

Using the water expertise of IDE Technologies, we identify, develop, design, finance, own, and operate large water infrastructure projects worldwide. With experience dating back to the late 20th century, IDE is a world pioneer in developing PPP projects in the water field. World-class understanding in all disciplines in the water technology market, and unrivalled experience in operation and maintenance of large and complexwater infrastructure projects, allow IDE to optimize the most effective and secured solution to its valued offtakers. A Partnership approach at the forefront of our activities allows efficient risk allocation and the delivery of the most advanced, robust, and most importantly – cost-effective outcome to the of-taker in the shortest possible time.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Investigate innovative methods that utilize renewable energy sources to minimize environmental impact and ensure a sustainable freshwater supply.
Examine advanced desalination technologies like reverse osmosis and multi-effect distillation that integrate eco-friendly practices for efficient water purification.
Identify current challenges such as energy consumption and brine disposal while outlining future research directions to enhance sustainability in desalination processes.
